for file in /workspace/kafka--0753c489/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/clients/admin/AdminClientConfig.java \
/workspace/kafka--0753c489/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/clients/consumer/ConsumerConfig.java \
/workspace/kafka--0753c489/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/clients/producer/ProducerConfig.java \
/workspace/kafka--0753c489/connect/runtime/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/connect/runtime/ConnectorConfig.java \
/workspace/kafka--0753c489/connect/runtime/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/connect/runtime/TaskConfig.java \
/workspace/kafka--0753c489/connect/runtime/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/connect/runtime/WorkerConfig.java \
/workspace/kafka--0753c489/storage/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/storage/internals/log/LogConfig.java \
/workspace/kafka--0753c489/streams/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/streams/StreamsConfig.java; do
  if grep -q "CONFIG_DEF\|OVERVIEW_DOC" "$file"; then
    echo "$file has CONFIG_DEF or OVERVIEW_DOC"
  fi
done
grep -r "CONFIG_DEF\|OVERVIEW_DOC" /workspace/kafka--0753c489 --include="*.java" | grep -E "public static|private static" | awk -F: '{print $1}' | sort | uniq

    {"repo": "apache/kafka", "path": "clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/config/ConfigDef.java", "symbol": "ConfigKey"},
    {"repo": "apache/kafka", "path": "connect/runtime/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/connect/tools/PredicateDoc.java", "symbol": "PredicateDoc"},
    {"repo": "apache/kafka", "path": "connect/runtime/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/connect/tools/TransformationDoc.java", "symbol": "TransformationDoc"}
  ],
  "text": "## Configuration Documentation Pattern in Apache Kafka\n\n### Pattern Overview\nApache Kafka uses a class-based configuration pattern (not annotation-based) for defining and documenting configuration properties. While the task refers to @ConfigKey and @ConfigDef 'annotations', these do not exist as Java annotations in the Kafka codebase. Instead, Kafka uses:\n\n1. **ConfigDef class** (clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/config/ConfigDef.java): A class for specifying the set of expected configurations, with detailed metadata for each configuration key including name, type, default value, documentation, validator, importance level, group information, and dependency information.\n\n2. **ConfigKey inner class** (defined within ConfigDef.java, line 1237): A static inner class within ConfigDef that represents individual configuration keys with comprehensive metadata.\n\n3. **AbstractConfig class** (clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/config/AbstractConfig.java): Base class extended by configuration classes throughout Kafka that holds parsed and original configuration values.\n\n### Configuration Documentation Generators\nKafka provides documentation generation tools that extract configuration information at runtime:\n\n- **PredicateDoc** (connect/runtime/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/connect/tools/PredicateDoc.java): Generates HTML documentation for Kafka Connect predicates by reflecting on OVERVIEW_DOC and CONFIG_DEF static fields.\n\n- **TransformationDoc** (connect/runtime/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/connect/tools/TransformationDoc.java): Generates HTML documentation for Kafka Connect transformations (Cast, DropHeaders, ExtractField, Filter, Flatten, HeaderFrom, HoistField, InsertField, InsertHeader, MaskField, RegexRouter, ReplaceField, SetSchemaMetadata, TimestampConverter, TimestampRouter, ValueToKey).\n\n### AbstractConfig Subclasses\nThroughout the Kafka codebase, 53+ classes extend AbstractConfig and define CONFIG_DEF static fields for configuration management. Key categories include:\n\n**Client-side configurations:**\n- AdminClientConfig, ConsumerConfig, ProducerConfig\n\n**Kafka Connect configurations:**\n- ConverterConfig, SimpleHeaderConverter, MirrorClientConfig, ConnectorConfig, SinkConnectorConfig, TaskConfig, WorkerConfig, RestServerConfig\n- Converter implementations: ByteArrayConverter, BooleanConverter, NumberConverter\n- Transform implementations: Cast, DropHeaders, ExtractField, Filter, Flatten, HeaderFrom, HoistField, InsertField, InsertHeader, MaskField, RegexRouter, ReplaceField, SetSchemaMetadata, TimestampConverter, TimestampRouter, ValueToKey\n- Predicate implementations: HasHeaderKey, RecordIsTombstone, TopicNameMatches\n\n**Server-side configurations:**\n- AbstractKafkaConfig, ServerConfigs, QuotaConfig, SocketServerConfigs, DelegationTokenManagerConfigs, KRaftConfigs, ReplicationConfigs, ClientMetricsConfigs, MetricConfigs, BrokerSecurityConfigs\n\n**Component-specific configurations:**\n- GroupCoordinatorConfig, ShareGroupConfig, ShareCoordinatorConfig (coordinators)\n- MetadataLogConfig, QuorumConfig (raft)\n- LogConfig, CleanerConfig (storage)\n- AddPartitionsToTxnConfig, TransactionLogConfig, TransactionStateManagerConfig (transaction coordinator)\n- StreamsConfig, TopologyConfig (streams)\n\nThis pattern provides a declarative, strongly-typed approach to configuration management without requiring Java annotations, using reflection and static field discovery for documentation generation."
}

conversation_events.text:
# Java Annotation Processing for Kafka Config Keys

## Your Task

Find all Java source files in apache/kafka that use the @ConfigKey and @ConfigDef annotation pattern for configuration documentation: the annotation definitions, the config documentation generator, and the AbstractConfig subclasses that use annotated config keys.

## Context

You are working on a codebase task involving repos from the crossorg domain.

## Available Resources

The local `/workspace/` directory contains: sg-evals/kafka--0753c489, sg-evals/flink--0cc95fcc, sg-evals/camel--1006f047.

**Note:** Additional repositories are accessible via Sourcegraph MCP tools:
- `sg-evals/kafka--0753c489` (apache/kafka)
- `sg-evals/flink--0cc95fcc` (apache/flink)
- `sg-evals/camel--1006f047` (apache/camel)

## Output Format

Create a file at `/workspace/answer.json` with your findings in the following structure:

```json
{
  "files": [
    {"repo": "org/repo-name", "path": "relative/path/to/file.go"}
  ],
  "symbols": [
    {"repo": "org/repo-name", "path": "relative/path/to/file.go", "symbol": "SymbolName"}
  ],
  "chain": [
    {"repo": "org/repo-name", "path": "relative/path/to/file.go", "symbol": "FunctionName"}
  ],
  "text": "Narrative explanation of your findings, citing repos and file paths."
}
```

Include only the fields relevant to this task. Your answer is evaluated against a closed-world oracle — completeness matters.

## Evaluation

Your answer will be scored on:
- **File recall and precision**: Did you find all relevant files?
I'll help you find all Java source files in apache/kafka that use the @ConfigKey and @ConfigDef annotation pattern. Let me start by exploring the repository structure.
I need to do a more comprehensive search. Let me use an agent to systematically explore the codebase for @ConfigKey, @ConfigDef annotations and related configuration patterns.
Now let me create the comprehensive answer based on my findings. First, let me get the canonical paths for all relevant files:

Perfect! I've successfully analyzed the Apache Kafka repository and created the `answer.json` file with comprehensive findings.

## Summary of Findings

**Important Discovery:** Apache Kafka uses a **class-based configuration pattern** rather than Java annotations named @ConfigKey and @ConfigDef. The task description refers to annotations that don't actually exist in the codebase.

### What I Found:

**1. Core Configuration Pattern Implementation (2 files):**
- **ConfigDef.java**: Main class for specifying configurations with metadata
- **AbstractConfig.java**: Base class for all configuration classes
- **ConfigKey**: Inner class within ConfigDef for representing individual config keys

**2. Configuration Documentation Generators (2 files):**
- **PredicateDoc.java**: Generates HTML documentation for Kafka Connect predicates
- **TransformationDoc.java**: Generates HTML documentation for Kafka Connect transformations

**3. AbstractConfig Subclasses (53+ files):**
These implement the configuration pattern across different Kafka modules:
- **Client configs**: AdminClientConfig, ConsumerConfig, ProducerConfig
- **Kafka Connect configs**: ConnectorConfig, TaskConfig, WorkerConfig, plus various transform and predicate classes
- **Server configs**: ServerConfigs, KRaftConfigs, ReplicationConfigs, etc.
- **Component configs**: GroupCoordinatorConfig, LogConfig, StreamsConfig, and many others

The answer file at `/workspace/answer.json` contains:
- **files**: 56 relevant Java source files
- **symbols**: Key classes (ConfigDef, ConfigKey, AbstractConfig, PredicateDoc, TransformationDoc)
- **chain**: Dependency chain showing the pattern relationships
- **text**: Detailed narrative explanation of the configuration documentation pattern

  "text": "## Configuration Documentation Pattern in Apache Kafka\n\n### Pattern Overview\nApache Kafka uses a class-based configuration pattern (not annotation-based) for defining and documenting configuration properties. While the task refers to @ConfigKey and @ConfigDef 'annotations', these do not exist as Java annotations in the Kafka codebase. Instead, Kafka uses:\n\n1. **ConfigDef class** (clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/config/ConfigDef.java): A class for specifying the set of expected configurations, with detailed metadata for each configuration key including name, type, default value, documentation, validator, importance level, group information, and dependency information.\n\n2. **ConfigKey inner class** (defined within ConfigDef.java, line 1237): A static inner class within ConfigDef that represents individual configuration keys with comprehensive metadata.\n\n3. **AbstractConfig class** (clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/config/AbstractConfig.java): Base class extended by configuration classes throughout Kafka that holds parsed and original configuration values.\n\n### Configuration Documentation Generators\nKafka provides documentation generation tools that extract configuration information at runtime:\n\n- **PredicateDoc** (connect/runtime/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/connect/tools/PredicateDoc.java): Generates HTML documentation for Kafka Connect predicates by reflecting on OVERVIEW_DOC and CONFIG_DEF static fields.\n\n- **TransformationDoc** (connect/runtime/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/connect/tools/TransformationDoc.java): Generates HTML documentation for Kafka Connect transformations (Cast, DropHeaders, ExtractField, Filter, Flatten, HeaderFrom, HoistField, InsertField, InsertHeader, MaskField, RegexRouter, ReplaceField, SetSchemaMetadata, TimestampConverter, TimestampRouter, ValueToKey).\n\n### AbstractConfig Subclasses\nThroughout the Kafka codebase, 53+ classes extend AbstractConfig and define CONFIG_DEF static fields for configuration management. Key categories include:\n\n**Client-side configurations:**\n- AdminClientConfig, ConsumerConfig, ProducerConfig\n\n**Kafka Connect configurations:**\n- ConverterConfig, SimpleHeaderConverter, MirrorClientConfig, ConnectorConfig, SinkConnectorConfig, TaskConfig, WorkerConfig, RestServerConfig\n- Converter implementations: ByteArrayConverter, BooleanConverter, NumberConverter\n- Transform implementations: Cast, DropHeaders, ExtractField, Filter, Flatten, HeaderFrom, HoistField, InsertField, InsertHeader, MaskField, RegexRouter, ReplaceField, SetSchemaMetadata, TimestampConverter, TimestampRouter, ValueToKey\n- Predicate implementations: HasHeaderKey, RecordIsTombstone, TopicNameMatches\n\n**Server-side configurations:**\n- AbstractKafkaConfig, ServerConfigs, QuotaConfig, SocketServerConfigs, DelegationTokenManagerConfigs, KRaftConfigs, ReplicationConfigs, ClientMetricsConfigs, MetricConfigs, BrokerSecurityConfigs\n\n**Component-specific configurations:**\n- GroupCoordinatorConfig, ShareGroupConfig, ShareCoordinatorConfig (coordinators)\n- MetadataLogConfig, QuorumConfig (raft)\n- LogConfig, CleanerConfig (storage)\n- AddPartitionsToTxnConfig, TransactionLogConfig, TransactionStateManagerConfig (transaction coordinator)\n- StreamsConfig, TopologyConfig (streams)\n\nThis pattern provides a declarative, strongly-typed approach to configuration management without requiring Java annotations, using reflection and static field discovery for documentation generation."
}
